content.text:
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Web">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- Exclude the nested xUnit test project so the web app build does not compile test sources. -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="TestProject.Tests/**" />
    <None Remove="TestProject.Tests/**" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- SPA build integration.

       The frontend lives in /src (*.ts) and is compiled to /wwwroot/dist by the
       `npm run build` script (tsc + scripts/copy-assets.mjs). Since wwwroot/dist is
       gitignored, it must be (re)generated before the C# build so the static files
       are present for `UseStaticFiles`/publish. These targets wire npm into the
       MSBuild pipeline so `dotnet build` and `dotnet publish` are self-contained.

       NpmInstall is incremental: it only runs when package.json or package-lock.json
       change (or node_modules is absent). node_modules/.package-lock.json is npm's
       own stamp file and serves as the output/up-to-date marker. -->
  <Target Name="NpmInstall"
          Inputs="package.json;package-lock.json"
          Outputs="node_modules/.package-lock.json">
    <Exec Command="npm install" WorkingDirectory="$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)" />
  </Target>

  <!-- NpmBuild always runs (tsc is fast and honors noEmitOnError), but only after
       deps are in place. Runs before BeforeBuild so dist exists prior to CoreBuild
       and static-asset copy during publish. -->
  <Target Name="NpmBuild"
          DependsOnTargets="NpmInstall"
          BeforeTargets="BeforeBuild">
    <Exec Command="npm run build" WorkingDirectory="$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)" />
  </Target>

</Project>

content.text:
# Software Developer Test Project 2026

## Project Goals

- **Spend a few hours:** Put your best foot forward, submit a project that shows your best work. The evaluation will be code reviews and discussions of your implementation and architecture choices.

- **Download the empty project:** [Here](#). We have started you with an index.html file and an empty controller. When you are done you can zip it up and share it with the team for review either as a download link or zip attachment to an email. If you share the project by email keep in mind the zip has exe files inside it and Gmail and other virus scanners may block the email. To avoid issues, rename the extension to something like `MyProject.CHANGEME`

- **End-to-End Flow that Works:** Implement both server side and client side logic in a primitive end to end flow that runs without errors for a few small features. The key goal is to show a working end to end proof of concept with enough C# and JavaScript code that we can get a feel for your coding style and have some interesting classes to discuss in the code review.

  - **Simple is better:** The less boilerplate junk the better - if you can pick a barebones project type or delete all the default controllers and files you are not using it will help us focus on your code

  - **Focus on Code:** Spend most of your time actually writing code so that we can have a substantial work product to review. For the purpose of this exercise we really need to see original code and are not interested in seeing a lot of framework or template usage.

## File & Directory Browsing Single Page App

The task is to create a web service API that allows users to query the contents of a directory on the web server and a single page web app that can be used to search and browse folders and files. A server side home directory should be configurable via variable.

Client side, the application should be deep-linkable (the state of the UI should be kept in the URL). All of the UI work should be done client side via JavaScript that renders HTML - do not render HTML server side. Styling is not as important for this task, we are more concerned with functionality. Build the UI using vanilla JavaScript or TypeScript (without React, Angular, or other UI library).

The goal is to produce a functional proof-of-concept to let us see how you think. It is important to provide a decent body of code, so that we can talk about your logic and choices. It isn't important to make the UI "pretty".

The point of this exercise is to end up with a body of work that is big enough that our team can review it and evaluate your design and coding style and skills.

## Requirements

- Ensure your solution builds in Visual Studio (any version 2022 or newer is acceptable), Rider, VS Code, or command line SDK tools
- Web API that allows Browse and Search Files & Folders and returns JSON
- Deep linkable URL pattern
- SPA (Single Page App using JavaScript)
- Upload/download files from the browser
- Show file and folder counts and sizes for the current view
- Build the UI using vanilla JavaScript or TypeScript (without React, Angular, or other UI library)

## Bonus

- Entire component contained in a dialog widget, with a trigger element (button, etc)
- Delete, move, copy files and folders
- Highly performant

content.thinking:
Good, now I have a complete picture. Let me compile all my findings for the Backend .NET Web API (production) scouting topic.

Let me organize these findings comprehensively.

## Summary of Findings

### 1. Dead Code: Unused `_logger` field in FilesController.cs
- File: `Controllers/FilesController.cs`, lines 22, 31
- `private readonly ILogger<FilesController> _logger` is declared and assigned in the constructor, but never referenced anywhere in the controller.
- This is dead code — the field is stored but never used for logging or any other purpose.

### 2. Duplicate Code: Repeated 5-catch blocks across all 6 endpoints
- File: `Controllers/FilesController.cs`
- The same 5-catch block pattern (ArgumentException, UnauthorizedAccessException, DirectoryNotFoundException, FileNotFoundException, IOException) is repeated verbatim in Browse (lines 54-68), Search (lines 87-101), Upload (lines 117-131), Download (lines 150-164), Delete (lines 179-193), Move (lines 210-224), and Copy (lines 241-255) — 7 times total (not 6 as initially stated; Download has its catch wrapped around ResolveFullPath separately).
- Additionally, `DirectoryNotFoundException` and `FileNotFoundException` are subtypes of `IOException`, making those last 3 catch blocks redundant — the IOException catch at the end would catch them anyway.
- This is ~210 lines of duplicate catch blocks that could be consolidated into a single helper or a middleware-based error filter.

### 3. Performance: `new FileExtensionContentTypeProvider()` on every Download request
- File: `Controllers/FilesController.cs`, line 189
- A new `FileExtensionContentTypeProvider` is instantiated on every single Download request.
- This object is stateless and thread-safe; it should be cached as a static field or registered as a singleton.

### 4. Performance: Two directory enumerations in Browse
- File: `Services/FileService.cs`, lines 48-49
- `Directory.GetDirectories(full)` and `Directory.GetFiles(full)` are called separately, walking the directory twice.
- Using `Directory.EnumerateFileSystemEntries` or `new DirectoryInfo(full).EnumerateFileSystemInfos()` would do it in one pass.

### 5. Bug: `Path.GetDirectoryName(destination)!` null-forgiving operator
- File: `Services/FileService.cs`, line 180
- `Directory.CreateDirectory(Path.GetDirectoryName(destination)!);`
- If `destination` is a root path like `C:\` (or the root itself), `Path.GetDirectoryName` returns null, and the null-forgiving operator suppresses the warning — but then `Directory.CreateDirectory(null!)` will throw an unexpected `ArgumentNullException` (or `ArgumentException`).
- The destination should be validated to have a parent directory, or this should handle the null case.

### 6. TOCTOU race condition in Delete
- File: `Services/FileService.cs`, lines 157-163
- `if (Directory.Exists(path)) { Directory.Delete(path, recursive: true); } else { File.Delete(path); }`
- Between the existence check and the delete operation, the file/directory type could change (TOCTOU = time-of-check, time-of-use).
- Additionally, a file could be replaced with a symlink between the check and the delete.
- Should use `File.Delete` directly which handles both files and directories (well, actually `File.Delete` only deletes files — but for a more robust approach, could try one and fall back to the other).

Actually, let me reconsider: `Directory.Delete` will throw if path is a file, and `File.Delete` will throw if path is a directory. The TOCTOU here is that between the `Directory.Exists` check and the actual delete, the filesystem state could change. A better approach would be to try `File.Delete` first (it doesn't throw if file doesn't exist), then try `Directory.Delete`.

Actually wait, `File.Delete` doesn't throw if the file doesn't exist. And `Directory.Delete` throws if the directory doesn't exist. So:

Better approach:
```csharp
try {
    File.Delete(path); // No-op if file doesn't exist, throws if path is a directory
} catch (DirectoryNotFoundException) {
    // Already gone
} catch (IOException) when (Directory.Exists(path)) {
    Directory.Delete(path, recursive: true);
}
```

Hmm, actually `File.Delete` doesn't throw on directories; it succeeds silently on directories in .NET (or throws UnauthorizedAccessException on some platforms). Let me think again...

Actually in .NET Core / .NET 5+, `File.Delete` on a directory throws `UnauthorizedAccessException`. So you can't just call `File.Delete` on a directory. The TOCTOU issue is real but minor for this application.

### 7. Bug: Upload response path uses raw, unnormalized `path` query parameter
- File: `Controllers/FilesController.cs`, lines 138-139
- `return Ok(new { path = string.IsNullOrEmpty(path) ? file.FileName : $"{path}/{file.FileName}" });`
- The response path is built from the raw `path` query parameter without normalization. If the user passes `path=docs//sub` (double slash) or `path=docs/./sub`, the response would have a non-normalized path.
- Should use `_service.NormalizePath()` or similar, or at least use `Path.GetFullPath` equivalent relative logic.

### 8. Code Smell: Singleton FileService with no thread safety
- File: `Services/FileService.cs` — registered as singleton in `Program.cs` line 16
- The service is stateful (uses file system operations that have inherent races) but is registered as a singleton.
- While the service itself has no mutable instance state beyond the readonly `_root`, the file system operations are inherently not thread-safe. Concurrent uploads to the same path, concurrent delete/move, etc. could cause races.
- This is more of a design consideration than a bug, but worth noting.

### 9. Misleading Name: `Browse` method's `entries.Sort` is O(n log n) when data is already grouped
- Not really a bug but the sort function `CompareEntries` is called on the full list after appending all directories first, then all files. Since they're already grouped (dirs first, then files), this is fine — but it's not taking advantage of the known ordering.

### 10. Code Smell: `ComputeParent` in FileService.cs uses manual string manipulation
- File: `Services/FileService.cs`, lines 117-129
- Uses `relative.LastIndexOf('/')` and `Substring` to compute parent path. While this works for forward-slash paths (as guaranteed by `ToRelative`), it's reinventing what `Path.GetDirectoryName()` could do.

### 11. Code Smell: `FileServiceOptions.HomeDirectory` has default value "Home" but appsettings overrides to "SampleFiles"
- The default in code is "Home" but both appsettings files use "SampleFiles". The default is never actually used since both config files provide the value.
- This is minor but the default could be removed or changed to match what's actually used.

### 12. Tautological test: `FileServiceOptionsTests.HomeDirectory_CanBeSetToArbitraryStringValue`
- File: `TestProject.Tests/FileServiceOptionsTests.cs`, lines 25-32
- Tests that a string property can be set to various string values. This tests the language/compiler behavior, not application logic.

### 13. Tautological tests: `FileSystemModelsTests` value equality tests
- While not strictly tautological (they test the record equality semantics which are part of the contract), many of the FileSystemModelsTests are just verifying C# compiler behavior for records (value equality, sealed, property types).

### 14. Unused import: `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc.Infrastructure` in FilesControllerTests.cs
- Line 5 of FilesControllerTests.cs imports `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc.Infrastructure` but I don't see anything from that namespace being used in the test file.

### 15. Name/file mismatch: `FileServiceOptions` lives in root namespace `TestProject`, not in a dedicated folder
- The class is at `FileServiceOptions.cs` in the root, but its namespace is `TestProject` while all other production classes are in `TestProject.Controllers`, `TestProject.Services`, or `TestProject.Models`.
- This is inconsistent namespace organization.

### 16. The `FileService` constructor creates the home directory during construction
- File: `Services/FileService.cs`, line 23
- `Directory.CreateDirectory(root);` runs during construction. If the directory creation fails (permissions, path too long, etc.), the DI container would throw an exception at startup. This could be deferred to first use.

### 17. Missing null-check for `file` parameter in Upload
- File: `Services/FileService.cs`, line 137 (`UploadAsync`)
- The `file` parameter of `IFormFile` type is not null-checked. If a null is passed (malformed request), it would throw `NullReferenceException` at `file.FileName`.

### 18. `Search` catches exceptions per-directory but `Browse` does not
- `FileService.cs`: `Search` catches `UnauthorizedAccessException` and `DirectoryNotFoundException` per-directory during the recursive walk (lines 88-91), which is good for robustness.
- `Browse` does not have similar protection — if a directory has an inaccessible subdirectory, `Directory.GetDirectories` will throw and propagate up.
- However, `Browse` only lists immediate children (not recursive), so this is less of an issue.

### 19. `CopyDirectory` is recursive but not depth-limited
- `FileService.cs`, line 238-248: The `CopyDirectory` method is recursive without any depth limit. Deeply nested directories could cause a stack overflow.

### 20. `appsettings.json` and `appsettings.Development.json` are identical
- Both files have exactly the same content. The Development overrides file has no development-specific settings.

Now let me compile these findings into a structured JSON report.
